chapter.title: API Conventions
chapter.para: The Raptor API follows a small set of conventions for memory ownership, return values, callback registration and threading. This chapter summarises them and is particularly useful reading before writing language bindings.

listitem.para: A raptor_X_as_string() / raptor_X_as_counted_string() accessor such as raptor_uri_as_string() returns a pointer into the object that the object still owns. Do not free it, and do not use it after the object is freed.
A raptor_X_to_string() / raptor_X_to_counted_string() accessor such as raptor_uri_to_string() returns a newly allocated copy that the caller owns and must free with raptor_free_memory().

Reference-counted objects (raptor_uri, raptor_term) are shared by an internal usage count: raptor_uri_copy() / raptor_term_copy() add a reference and raptor_free_uri() / raptor_free_term() drop one; the object is destroyed when the last reference is freed. Free every object exactly as many times as you obtained a reference to it.
Most methods return int 0 on success and non-0 on failure.
Predicate helpers such as raptor_term_equals() return non-0 for true and 0 for false (not an error code).
raptor_X_compare() helpers return <0, 0 or >0 like strcmp().
Constructors and _as_ / _to_ accessors return a pointer, or NULL on failure.

section.title: Callback registration argument order
section.para: The order of the user_data and function pointer arguments to the callback registration functions is not uniform:

listitem.para: The parser and world handler setters take user_data before the function pointer, such as raptor_world_set_log_handler()(world, user_data, handler) and raptor_parser_set_statement_handler()(parser, user_data, handler).

The www setters and both uri_filter setters take user_data after the function pointer, such as raptor_www_set_content_type_handler()(www, handler, user_data) and raptor_parser_set_uri_filter()(parser, filter, user_data).

section.para: Both arguments are pointers, so a swap can compile cleanly; check the specific prototype.

section.title: Threading
section.para: Raptor has no internal locking. Use one raptor_world per thread and do not share a raptor_world, or any object created from it, across threads: ordinary operations mutate shared world state (the message buffer, the interned-URI tree, the blank node counter) with plain, non-atomic updates. Even a nominally immutable interned raptor_uri races its usage count if shared, so objects are not safe to pass between threads without external synchronisation.
